walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Bird Deck
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Wingspan set up. First, find the big deck of bird cards. These are the beautiful illustrated cards with birds on the front and a feather pattern on the back. The bonus cards are the smaller deck with a botanical leaf pattern, so don't mix them up. Give the bird deck a good shuffle and set it face down on the table near everyone.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bird deck and tray with face-up birds

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Bird Tray
walkthrough.text: Now grab the blue bird tray. This is the long plastic piece with slots for cards. Flip three bird cards face up from the top of the deck and lay them in the tray. These are the birds available for anyone to draft during the game. The rest of the deck stays face down right next to the tray.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three face-up birds displayed on the bird tray

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Food Supply
walkthrough.text: Next, let's set out the food tokens. You will see round tokens with different pictures on them. There are five types: invertebrates, which look like a caterpillar. Seeds, which look like a wheat stalk. Fish. Fruit, which look like berries. And rodents, which look like a little mouse. Put all of these in a shared pile in the middle of the table where everyone can reach them.
walkthrough.image_caption: Food token supply with all five types

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Egg Supply
walkthrough.text: Now find the egg miniatures. These are the adorable little pastel eggs in different colors. Blue, green, white, pink, and tan. Dump them all into a shared supply near the food tokens. The color of the eggs does not matter at all during gameplay. They are purely decorative, so grab whichever color you like when you need one.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pastel egg miniatures in the supply

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Birdfeeder
walkthrough.text: Here is the fun part. Grab the birdfeeder dice tower. This is the wooden tower that looks like a little birdhouse. Stand it up on the table. Now take the five custom wooden dice and drop them into the top of the tower. Let them tumble down. They will land in the tray at the bottom. These dice show different food icons and will be your main way to collect food during the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Birdfeeder dice tower assembled

walkthrough.title: Choose a Goal Board Side
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the goal board. This is the rectangular board with four spaces for goal tiles. It has two sides, and your group needs to pick one. The green side is more competitive. It awards points based on first, second, and third place for each goal. The blue side is more relaxed. It gives one point for each qualifying item you have, up to five points. For your first game, either side works, but the blue side tends to feel a bit friendlier.
walkthrough.image_caption: Goal board showing the blue scoring side

walkthrough.title: Place the Goal Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the eight small square goal tiles. These are double-sided, so do not peek at them. Give them a quick shuffle face down, then place one random tile on each of the four goal spaces on the board. It does not matter which side faces up. Put any leftover tiles back in the box without looking at them. Each tile shows what players will be scored on at the end of each round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Eight goal tiles spread out

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Bonus Deck
walkthrough.text: Last piece for the shared area. Find the bonus cards. These are smaller cards with a tan border and special scoring conditions on them, like Omnivore Expert or Wetland Specialist. Shuffle them into a face-down deck and set it on the table. Players will get some of these during setup, and a few bird powers let you draw more during the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Mats
walkthrough.text: Now let's get each player set up. Give everyone one player mat. This is the big beautiful board with three rows showing different landscapes. The top row is the forest, for gaining food. The middle row is the grassland, for laying eggs. And the bottom row is the wetland, for drawing bird cards. You will be placing birds along these rows during the game to build your engine.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat showing forest, grassland, and wetland rows

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Action Cubes
walkthrough.text: Each player also gets eight action cubes of one color. Everyone pick a color. You will use these cubes to mark which action you take on your turn. You start with eight in round one, then seven in round two, six in round three, and five in round four. You lose one each round because you place it on the goal board for scoring.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five sets of colored action cubes

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Now deal five random bird cards face down to each player. Also deal two random bonus cards to each player. Go ahead and look at your cards, but keep them hidden from other players if you want. You can also choose to play open-handed if your group prefers.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Food
walkthrough.text: Each player also takes five food tokens from the supply. You get one of each type: one invertebrate, one seed, one fish, one fruit, and one rodent. Place them next to your player mat where everyone can see them. Your food supply is always public information.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five food types — one of each for starting supply

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Here is the first real decision of the game. You have five bird cards and five food tokens. You can keep up to five birds, but for each bird you keep, you must discard one food token back to the supply. So if you keep three birds, you return three food and keep two food. If you keep one bird, you keep four food. Look at the food costs in the upper left of each bird card. Try to keep birds whose food costs match the food you are keeping. Also look at your two bonus cards. You will keep one bonus card and discard the other. Your bonus card might influence which birds you want to keep.

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Almost done with setup. Pick a random person to go first and give them the first player token. This is the round token with the beautiful bird illustration on it. Play will go clockwise from the first player. The first player token will rotate clockwise at the end of each round, so everyone gets a turn going first. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.
walkthrough.image_caption: First-player token with bird illustration

summary.title: Shuffle the Bird Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 170 bird cards into one face-down deck
Place centrally where all players can reach

summary.title: Set Up the Bird Tray
bullets: Place bird tray next to deck
Reveal 3 face-up bird cards into the tray slots

summary.title: Lay Out the Food Supply
bullets: Place all 103 food tokens in a shared central supply

summary.title: Set Out the Egg Supply
bullets: Place all 75 egg miniatures in a shared supply
Egg color is cosmetic only — grab any color

summary.title: Assemble the Birdfeeder
bullets: Stand up the birdfeeder dice tower
Toss all 5 food dice into the top — they land in the tray

summary.title: Choose a Goal Board Side
bullets: Place goal board on table — choose a side
Green side: 1st/2nd/3rd place competition (more competitive)
Blue side: 1 point per qualifying item, max 5 (friendlier)

summary.title: Place the Goal Tiles
bullets: Shuffle 8 goal tiles face-down (double-sided, don't peek)
Place 1 random tile on each of the 4 goal board spaces
Return extras to box

summary.title: Prepare the Bonus Deck
bullets: Shuffle 26 bonus cards into a face-down deck
Place on table near bird deck

summary.title: Hand Out Player Mats
bullets: Give each player 1 player mat
Forest (top row) = Gain Food
Grassland (middle row) = Lay Eggs
Wetland (bottom row) = Draw Bird Cards

summary.title: Hand Out Action Cubes
bullets: Each player takes 8 action cubes of one color
Cube count decreases each round: 8, 7, 6, 5
One cube placed on goal board per round for scoring

summary.title: Deal Starting Cards
bullets: Deal 5 random bird cards to each player (face-down)
Deal 2 random bonus cards to each player
Hands may be private or public by group choice

summary.title: Collect Starting Food
bullets: Each player takes 5 food tokens from supply
1 invertebrate, 1 seed, 1 fish, 1 fruit, 1 rodent
Food supply is always public information

summary.title: Choose Your Starting Hand
bullets: For each bird card kept (up to 5), discard 1 food token
Example: keep 3 birds, keep 2 food
Choose 1 of 2 bonus cards to keep — discard the other
Tip: match kept birds' food costs to your remaining food

summary.title: Select First Player
bullets: Randomly select first player — give them the first-player token
Play proceeds clockwise
Token rotates clockwise each round

walkthrough.title: The Four Rounds
walkthrough.text: Wingspan is played over four rounds. In each round, everyone takes turns going clockwise. You take one action per turn by placing an action cube. In round one you get eight turns, then seven, then six, then five. The game gets shorter each round, but your bird powers get stronger because you have more birds on your mat. When everyone has used all their cubes, the round ends.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat showing three habitat rows

walkthrough.title: Your Four Action Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you pick one of four actions. You can see them on the left side of your player mat. First, you can play a bird from your hand onto your mat. Second, you can gain food from the birdfeeder, which uses the forest row. Third, you can lay eggs on your birds, which uses the grassland row. Fourth, you can draw bird cards, which uses the wetland row. You place your action cube to show which action you chose, then resolve it. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action: Play a Bird
walkthrough.text: To play a bird, first check three things. One: does the bird's habitat icon match a row that still has space? Birds show forest, grassland, wetland, or sometimes multiple habitats. Two: can you pay its food cost? The food icons are in the upper left corner of the card. Three: can you pay the egg cost? The first column of each row is free, columns two and three cost one egg, and columns four and five cost two eggs. If you can afford everything, place your action cube above the column where you are playing the bird. Pay the food from your supply and discard any required eggs from birds already on your mat. Then place the bird on the leftmost open slot in that row. If the bird says When Played, you may use that power right now. That is the only time it triggers.

walkthrough.title: Action: Gain Food
walkthrough.text: To gain food, place your action cube on the leftmost open slot in the forest row on your mat. The slot shows how many dice you can take from the birdfeeder. For each die you take, remove it from the birdfeeder tray and collect one matching food token from the supply. If a die shows two icons with a slash between them, you pick one of the two, not both. Some later slots also let you trade a bird card from your hand for one extra food. After collecting your food, move your cube to the right and activate any brown bird powers in the forest row, one at a time from right to left. Each brown power is optional.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five custom food dice showing different food types

walkthrough.title: Action: Lay Eggs
walkthrough.text: To lay eggs, place your cube on the leftmost open slot in the grassland row. The slot tells you how many eggs you can lay. Take egg miniatures from the supply and place them on any of your birds. Each bird has an egg limit shown by small egg icons on the card. You can spread eggs across multiple birds or pile them on one bird, as long as you do not exceed any bird's limit. If you have more eggs to place than you have room for, the extras are simply lost. Some later slots let you spend one food token to lay one bonus egg. Then activate brown powers in the grassland row from right to left.
walkthrough.image_caption: Colorful egg miniatures from the supply

walkthrough.title: Action: Draw Bird Cards
walkthrough.text: To draw bird cards, place your cube on the leftmost open slot in the wetland row. The slot tells you how many cards to draw. You can draw from the three face-up cards on the bird tray or from the top of the deck, in any combination. There is no hand limit in Wingspan, so draw away. When you take a face-up card, do not refill the tray until your entire turn is done. Some later slots let you trade one egg for one extra card draw. Then activate brown powers in the wetland row from right to left.

walkthrough.title: Bird Powers Explained
walkthrough.text: Bird cards can have three types of powers, and the color tells you when they trigger. White or no-color powers say When Played. These happen once, right when you play the bird, and never again. Brown powers say When Activated. These fire every time you use that habitat row for an action. You resolve them from right to left, and each one is optional. Pink powers say Once Between Turns. These can trigger on another player's turn based on their actions. You can only use each pink power once between your own turns. When someone triggers your pink power, speak up and resolve it. Powers are always optional. You never have to use one if you do not want to.

walkthrough.title: Birdfeeder Management
walkthrough.text: A quick note on managing the birdfeeder. When you take dice out for food, they stay outside the tower until the feeder needs to be refilled. If the birdfeeder tray is completely empty, toss all five dice back in. There is also a special rule: if all the remaining dice in the tray show the same face, and you are about to gain food, you may choose to reroll all five dice first. This also applies if there is only one die left. The split-face die counts as its own unique face for this rule.
walkthrough.image_caption: Birdfeeder dice tower — toss dice in from the top

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: When every player has placed all their action cubes, the round ends. Four things happen in order. First, everyone picks up all their action cubes from the player mat. Second, you score the current round's end-of-round goal. Each player places one action cube on the goal board to mark their score. This is why you lose one cube each round. Third, discard all face-up birds on the tray and refill it with three new cards from the deck. Fourth, pass the first-player token clockwise. Then start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Goal Scoring Details
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about how the end-of-round goals work, because it depends on which side of the board you chose. On the green side, it is a competition. Everyone counts their qualifying items for the goal, and whoever has the most gets first place points, second most gets second place, and so on. If players tie, both go in the tied position and the next position is skipped. The tied players split the points of the tied place and the next place, rounding down. On the blue side, it is simpler. You just count your qualifying items and score one point each, up to a maximum of five. Either way, you must place your cube even if you score zero.
walkthrough.image_caption: Goal board with scoring track

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after round four. Now it is time to add up your score. There are six categories. First, add the printed point values on all the bird cards in your tableau. Second, score your bonus card. Third, add your end-of-round goal points from the goal board. Fourth, count one point for each egg sitting on your birds. Fifth, count one point for each food token cached on your birds. Sixth, count one point for each card tucked under your birds. Add all six categories together. The highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most leftover food tokens wins. If still tied, you share the victory.

summary.title: The Four Rounds
bullets: Game = 4 rounds, players take turns clockwise
Round 1: 8 turns, Round 2: 7, Round 3: 6, Round 4: 5
Fewer turns per round, but bird power chains grow stronger

summary.title: Your Four Action Choices
bullets: Play a Bird — place a bird card from hand onto your mat
Gain Food — take food from birdfeeder (Forest row)
Lay Eggs — place eggs on your birds (Grassland row)
Draw Bird Cards — draw from tray or deck (Wetland row)

summary.title: Action: Play a Bird
bullets: Check habitat match (forest/grassland/wetland)
Pay food cost (icons in upper-left of card)
Pay egg cost: Column 1 free, Columns 2-3 = 1 egg, Columns 4-5 = 2 eggs
2-for-1 conversion: any 2 food = any 1 food (play bird only)
Place bird on leftmost open slot, resolve WHEN PLAYED power if any

summary.title: Action: Gain Food
bullets: Place cube on leftmost open Forest slot
Take dice from birdfeeder = gain matching food tokens
Split-face die: choose 1 of 2 icons, not both
Optional: discard 1 bird card for 1 extra food (if slot allows)
Activate brown powers in Forest row, right to left

summary.title: Action: Lay Eggs
bullets: Place cube on leftmost open Grassland slot
Lay eggs equal to slot value onto any birds on your mat
Each bird has a printed egg limit — do not exceed
Optional: pay 1 food for 1 extra egg (if slot allows)
Activate brown powers in Grassland row, right to left

summary.title: Action: Draw Bird Cards
bullets: Place cube on leftmost open Wetland slot
Draw cards from face-up tray and/or deck (any mix)
No hand limit — do not refill tray until end of turn
Optional: discard 1 egg for 1 extra card (if slot allows)
Activate brown powers in Wetland row, right to left

summary.title: Bird Powers Explained
bullets: WHEN PLAYED (white) — triggers once when bird is first played
WHEN ACTIVATED (brown) — triggers every time you use that row, right to left
ONCE BETWEEN TURNS (pink) — may trigger on opponents' turns, max once per cycle
All powers are always optional

summary.title: Birdfeeder Management
bullets: Tray completely empty = reroll all 5 dice into tower
All remaining dice show same face (even 1 die) AND gaining food = may reroll first
Split-face die counts as its own unique face

summary.title: End of Round
bullets: Remove all action cubes from player mats
Score round goal — each player places 1 cube on goal board
Refresh bird tray — discard all face-up birds, deal 3 new
Rotate first-player token clockwise, begin next round

summary.title: Goal Scoring Details
bullets: Green side: rank by qualifying items, award 1st/2nd/3rd place points
Ties on green: share tied + next place points, round down
Blue side: 1 point per qualifying item, max 5 points
Must place cube even if scoring 0

summary.title: Game End and Final Scoring
bullets: Game ends after Round 4
Score: bird points + bonus card + round goals + eggs + cached food + tucked cards
Each egg, cached food, and tucked card = 1 point
Tie-break: most unused food tokens, then shared victory

walkthrough.title: Starting Hand Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your very first decision shapes your whole game. When you are choosing which birds and food to keep, think about what you can actually play in the first two turns. A hand full of expensive five-point birds sounds great, but if you cannot afford to play any of them until turn four, you have wasted your early game. Keep two or three affordable birds and enough food to play them immediately. Look at your bonus card too. If it rewards wetland birds, lean toward keeping wetland birds and planning your early game around that row.

walkthrough.title: Build One Engine First
walkthrough.text: In the early game, resist the urge to spread yourself thin across all three habitats. Focus on building one reliable row first. Many experienced players start with the forest, because food is the most important resource early on. You need food to play birds, and birds are what make everything else work. Get two or three cheap birds into the forest so that every time you gain food, you also trigger their brown powers. This creates a compounding effect that gets stronger each time you use that row.

walkthrough.title: Evaluating Bird Cards
walkthrough.text: Not all birds are created equal, and point value alone does not tell the whole story. A one-point bird with a powerful brown power that fires every turn can earn you far more than a seven-point bird with no useful ability. When evaluating a bird, consider four things. What is the food cost? How often will its power trigger? Does it align with your bonus card or current round goal? And can you actually play it given your food and egg situation? The best birds are cheap, have strong brown powers, and fit your existing strategy.

walkthrough.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
walkthrough.text: Each round gets shorter, so your strategy should evolve. In round one, with eight turns, focus on building your engine. Play birds, establish your food pipeline, and set up for the first end-of-round goal. In round two, your engine should start paying off. Keep building, but start thinking about egg production for column costs. Round three is the pivot point. With only six turns, you need to shift from building to scoring. Make sure your egg supply can support playing birds into the later columns. Round four is your sprint finish. Only five turns, so make each one count toward points.

walkthrough.title: The Egg Economy
walkthrough.text: Eggs serve double duty in Wingspan, and new players often underestimate them. First, you need eggs to play birds into columns two through five. If you do not lay eggs early enough, you will get stuck with birds you cannot play. Second, each egg on a bird at game end is worth one point. A grassland row with three or four birds generating eggs every turn can be a massive point engine. Plan your egg production before you need the eggs, not after you are already stuck.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Card Alignment
walkthrough.text: Your bonus card is a secret scoring multiplier that can swing the game by five to ten points. The key is to let it guide your bird selection without becoming a slave to it. If your bonus card rewards cavity-nesting birds, look for cavity nesters when drafting, but do not skip a great bird just because it has a bowl nest. The best approach is to treat your bonus card as a tiebreaker. When two birds seem equally good, pick the one that helps your bonus. And if you gain a second bonus card during the game from a When Played power, even better.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players do not rely on just one source of points. They collect points from every category. Bird card points, bonus card points, end-of-round goals, eggs, cached food, and tucked cards all add up. A player with twenty points in birds but zero in everything else will lose to someone with fifteen in birds but also five from eggs, four from goals, three from tucked cards, and three from cached food. Think about which scoring categories you are naturally building toward and make sure you are not leaving any on the table.

summary.title: Starting Hand Strategy
bullets: Keep 2-3 affordable birds you can play in turns 1-2
Match food tokens to kept birds' food costs
Consider bonus card synergy when choosing birds
Avoid keeping 4-5 expensive birds with only 0-1 food

summary.title: Build One Engine First
bullets: Focus on 1 habitat row first (usually Forest for food generation)
Place 2-3 cheap birds with brown powers early
Compounding effect: more birds in a row = stronger each use
Forest first then food to play more birds then snowball

summary.title: Evaluating Bird Cards
bullets: Food cost — cheaper = easier to deploy early
Power frequency — brown powers trigger repeatedly (high value)
Goal/bonus alignment — does it help your bonus or round goal?
Playability — can you afford the food AND egg cost right now?

summary.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
bullets: Round 1 (8 turns): engine-building, play cheap birds
Round 2 (7 turns): engine producing, balance with egg prep
Round 3 (6 turns): pivot from building to scoring
Round 4 (5 turns): sprint, maximize points per action

summary.title: The Egg Economy
bullets: Eggs are currency (play birds in columns 2-5) AND points (1 VP each)
Common trap: ignoring eggs until you can't play birds
Plan grassland production early, before you need eggs
Strong grassland row with 3-4 birds = huge point engine

summary.title: Bonus Card Alignment
bullets: Bonus cards can swing 5-10 points
Let bonus card guide bird selection, but don't tunnel on it
Use as tiebreaker: between 2 equal birds, pick the one that fits
WHEN PLAYED powers that grant extra bonus cards = high value

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring
bullets: All 6 scoring categories matter — don't tunnel on bird points alone
Bird points + Bonus + Goals + Eggs + Cached food + Tucked cards
A balanced 15+5+4+3+3 beats a one-dimensional 20+0+0+0+0
Check which categories your birds naturally support

entries.definition: A power that triggers once, immediately when the bird is first placed on your player mat. It never triggers again after that initial play. Shown in white or with no colored banner.
A power that triggers each time you use the habitat action matching that bird's row. Brown powers activate from right to left during the habitat action. Each brown power is optional.

A power that may trigger during another player's turn based on their actions. You can only use each pink power once between each of your own turns. Announce to opponents what triggers your pink power.
Place a food token from the general supply onto a bird card. Cached food cannot be spent as a resource. Each cached food token is worth one victory point at end of game.

Take a bird card and slide it partially under another bird card on your mat. Tucked cards are not in play and have no powers. Each tucked card is worth one victory point at end of game. This represents flocking behavior.

When activated, look at the top card of the bird deck. If its wingspan is less than a specified value, tuck it behind the predator bird. If the wingspan is too large, discard the card. This represents hunting smaller birds.
A power that lets you tuck bird cards from your hand or the deck behind a bird. Each tucked card is worth one point at end of game. Some flocking powers require you to discard a resource first.
Some bird powers allow laying extra eggs, either on themselves or on other birds. These are in addition to the normal Lay Eggs action. Egg limits on birds still apply.
Some bird powers let you draw additional bird cards from the deck or tray. This is in addition to the normal Draw Bird Cards action. There is no hand limit.
Some bird powers grant food from the birdfeeder or directly from the supply. Gaining food from the supply does not require taking a die from the birdfeeder.
Some brown powers benefit all players at the table, though the bird's owner typically receives an extra benefit. When you activate such a power, announce it so opponents can take their benefit too.

The top row of the player mat. Used for the Gain Food action. When you place an action cube here, you take dice from the birdfeeder and collect matching food tokens. Forest birds often have food-related brown powers.

The middle row of the player mat. Used for the Lay Eggs action. When you place an action cube here, you lay eggs on your birds up to the slot's value. Grassland birds often have egg-related brown powers.

The bottom row of the player mat. Used for the Draw Bird Cards action. When you place an action cube here, you draw cards from the face-up tray or bird deck. Wetland birds often have card-draw brown powers.

A bird that shows more than one habitat icon in its upper left corner. When playing this bird, you choose which of its listed habitats to place it in. Once placed, it stays in that row for the rest of the game.
One of the five food types. Shown as a green caterpillar icon on dice and tokens. Represents insects, worms, and other invertebrates that birds eat.
One of the five food types. Shown as an orange wheat or grain icon on dice and tokens. Represents seeds, grains, and plant matter.
One of the five food types. Shown as a blue fish icon on dice and tokens. Represents aquatic prey.
One of the five food types. Shown as a pink or red berry icon on dice and tokens. Represents berries and other fruit.
One of the five food types. Shown as a gray mouse icon on dice and tokens. Represents small mammals that predatory birds eat.
A food icon on a bird card that can be paid with any one of the five food types. Shown as a multicolored or rainbow icon.
A crossed-out circle icon on a bird card meaning the bird has zero food cost to play. These birds are free to place if you have the egg cost covered.

When playing a bird only, you may spend any two food tokens as if they were any one food token of your choice. This conversion is not available during other actions or when paying costs from bird powers.
A food die showing two different food icons separated by a diagonal line. When you take this die from the birdfeeder, choose one of the two food types. You do not get both.
The maximum number of eggs a bird card can hold, shown by small egg icons on the card. You cannot place more eggs on a bird than its limit allows. Any excess eggs from an action are lost.

The cost in eggs to play a bird in columns 2 through 5 of your player mat. Columns 2 and 3 cost one egg; columns 4 and 5 cost two eggs. Column 1 is free. Discard eggs from any birds on your mat to pay.
At game end, each egg on your bird cards is worth one victory point. Eggs are a significant scoring source, especially when combined with high-egg-limit birds in the grassland row.
One of the four nest types. Shown as a flat platform icon beneath the bird's point value. Used for nest-related end-of-round goals and bonus cards.
One of the four nest types. Shown as a cup or bowl icon beneath the bird's point value. The most common nest type in the game.
One of the four nest types. Shown as a tree hole icon beneath the bird's point value. Cavity nesters use holes in trees or structures.
One of the four nest types. Shown as a grass clump icon beneath the bird's point value. Ground nesters build nests on or near the ground.

A special wild nest type shown as a star icon. These birds build unusual nests that do not fit the four standard types. Star nests count as any nest type for goals and bonus cards, making them very flexible.

Special scoring cards dealt during setup (keep one of two) and sometimes gained via bird powers. Each bonus card awards end-game points for meeting a specific condition, such as having birds with specific food types, nest types, or habitats.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with a wingspan over 65 centimeters. Rewards playing large, high-wingspan birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds worth less than 3 victory points. Rewards playing many small, efficient utility birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with tucked cards. Pairs well with flocking birds and predators that tuck cards.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that have the When Activated brown power. Rewards filling rows with engine birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat a specific food type. Rewards specializing in one food type.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with geographic range icons matching specified continents on their cards.
Bonus card. Score points for birds in each habitat, rewarding spreading birds across all three rows rather than concentrating in one.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that have a specific nest type. Rewards collecting birds with matching nest icons.
Bonus card. Score points for birds worth 5 or more victory points. Rewards playing expensive, high-value birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat fish. Rewards building around fish-eating birds and the fish food economy.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat only invertebrates. Rewards specializing in invertebrate-eating birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds specifically in the forest habitat. Rewards a heavy forest build strategy.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with a wingspan of 65 centimeters or greater.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with cavity nests. Pairs well with cavity nesters in any habitat.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat multiple different food types. The more diverse a bird's diet, the better it fits this bonus.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with a specific color trait or feature.
Bonus card. Score points for birds with platform nests. Pairs well with platform nesters such as raptors and large waterbirds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds in the grassland habitat. Rewards building a strong grassland egg engine.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat rodents. Rewards building around predatory rodent-eating birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds that eat fruit. Rewards collecting fruit-eating birds.
Bonus card. Score points for birds in the wetland habitat. Rewards building a strong wetland card-draw engine.

Each round ends with a goal scored by all players. Goals measure things like birds in a specific habitat, eggs in a specific nest type, or sets of items. One action cube per player is placed on the goal board each round to track scores.
End-of-round goal. Count the number of bird cards in your forest habitat row. Scored by rank (green) or per-item (blue) depending on board side.
End-of-round goal. Count the number of bird cards in your grassland habitat row.
End-of-round goal. Count the number of bird cards in your wetland habitat row.
End-of-round goal. Count the total number of eggs on bird cards in your forest habitat row.
End-of-round goal. Count the total number of eggs on bird cards in your grassland habitat row.
End-of-round goal. Count the total number of eggs on bird cards in your wetland habitat row.
End-of-round goal. Count all birds on your mat that have the bowl nest icon.
End-of-round goal. Count all birds on your mat that have the cavity nest icon.
End-of-round goal. Count all birds on your mat that have the ground nest icon.
End-of-round goal. Count all birds on your mat that have the platform nest icon.
End-of-round goal. Count the total number of bird cards across all three of your habitat rows.
End-of-round goal. Count complete sets of eggs across all three habitats. One set equals one egg in forest, one in grassland, and one in wetland. Score equals number of complete sets.

The physical wingspan measurement printed on bird cards, shown in centimeters at the right side of the card. Used primarily by predator powers to determine if a prey bird is small enough to be caught.
The food tokens required to play a bird card, shown in the upper left corner. Must be paid from your personal food supply, not from cached food on birds.
Wooden cubes used to mark your chosen action each turn. You start each round with a set number (8, 7, 6, 5) and one is permanently placed on the goal board each round.
The plastic tray that holds three face-up bird cards available for drafting. Refilled at end of turn for cards taken, and fully refreshed at end of each round.

The wooden dice tower used to hold and roll the five food dice. Dice are removed when players gain food. Refill all five dice when the tray is empty, or optionally when all remaining dice show the same face.
Your personal collection of bird cards placed on your player mat across the three habitat rows. Your tableau is the engine you build throughout the game.
A round token with a bird illustration. Given to the starting player and rotated clockwise at the end of each round.
The board where end-of-round goals are scored. Has two sides: Green (competitive, ranked placement) and Blue (independent, one point each up to five). Players place one action cube on it each round.
Used at game end to tally all six scoring categories: bird points, bonus card points, end-of-round goal points, eggs, cached food, and tucked cards.
Victory points printed on each bird card in your tableau. One of the six final scoring categories. Ranges from zero to nine points per bird.
Each food token placed on a bird card via a caching power is worth one victory point at game end. One of the six final scoring categories.
Each bird card tucked under another bird on your mat is worth one victory point at game end. One of the six final scoring categories. Accumulated via flocking and predator powers.

scenarios.title: Your First Round
scenarios.description: Learn all four actions with a balanced opening strategy

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Round 1
walkthrough.text: Everyone should be set up and ready to go. Each player has their player mat, their chosen starting birds and food, a bonus card, and eight action cubes. Round 1 gives you the most cubes you will have all game — eight — so you have plenty of room to experiment. The first player token is in front of Player 1 (Red). Let's walk through a full round together, one action at a time.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat ready with action cubes

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Red) Plays a Bird
walkthrough.text: Player 1 (Red) decides to play a bird on their very first turn. They look at their hand and pick a bird with a low food cost — say one that costs just a single invertebrate. To play it, Red places the bird card in column 1 of their forest row. Column 1 has no egg cost, so Red only needs to pay the food. Red spends one invertebrate token from their personal supply, returning it to the shared food supply. Done — the bird is now in the forest.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bird placed in column 1 of the forest row

walkthrough.title: What Just Happened
walkthrough.text: Red just used the Play a Bird action. This is the one action that does not use a habitat row — instead, you place one action cube on the bird card itself to remind you that you took an action this turn. The bird card shows its habitat icon, and you place it in the matching row. Column 1 is always free of egg costs. Columns 2 and 3 cost one egg each. Columns 4 and 5 cost two eggs each. If the bird has a white "When Played" power, it triggers right now, once, and never again. If it has a brown "When Activated" power, that power will fire every time you use this row's habitat action in the future.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Blue) Gains Food
walkthrough.text: Now it is Player 2 (Blue)'s turn. Blue wants to stock up on food for future birds. Blue places one action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in their forest row. Since Blue has no birds in the forest yet, that slot says "Gain 1 food." Blue looks at the birdfeeder dice tower. There are five dice sitting in the tray, each showing a food face. Blue picks up one die showing a seed icon, removes it from the tray, and takes one seed token from the supply.
walkthrough.image_caption: Birdfeeder with five custom food dice

walkthrough.title: How the Birdfeeder Works
walkthrough.text: The birdfeeder is your food market. When you take a die, you collect the matching food token from the supply. Some dice show a split face with two food types — you pick one of the two. Here is the important reroll rule: if all the remaining dice in the tray show the same face, or if the tray is completely empty, you scoop up all five dice and reroll them by dropping them back through the tower. This prevents the feeder from getting stuck on one food type. Keep this rule in mind — sometimes it is worth taking a die you do not need just to trigger a reroll for a better selection.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Green) Lays Eggs
walkthrough.text: Player 3 (Green) goes next. Green already played a bird during their setup choices — let's say they kept a grassland bird and played it on a previous hypothetical turn. For this walkthrough, imagine Green has one bird on their mat. Green places an action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in their grassland row. With no grassland birds yet boosting the action, the slot says "Lay 2 eggs." Green takes two egg miniatures from the supply and places them on any bird or birds they have. Each bird card shows a maximum egg limit — you can never exceed it.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pastel egg miniatures in the shared supply

walkthrough.title: Why Eggs Matter
walkthrough.text: Eggs serve two purposes in Wingspan, and both are important. First, they are currency. When you play a bird into columns 2 or 3, you must spend one egg. Columns 4 and 5 cost two eggs each. Without eggs, you cannot expand your engine beyond the first column. Second, every egg still sitting on a bird at the end of the game is worth one victory point. So eggs you do not spend become points. This dual role makes the Lay Eggs action valuable from start to finish.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 (Yellow) Draws Bird Cards
walkthrough.text: Last player this turn — Player 4 (Yellow). Yellow places an action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in their wetland row. The slot says "Draw 1 card." Yellow can draw from the three face-up cards in the bird tray, or draw blindly from the top of the deck. Yellow scans the tray, spots a bird with a useful power, and takes it into their hand. When you take from the tray, immediately refill the empty slot with a new card from the deck.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bird tray with face-up cards to choose from

walkthrough.title: Card Draw Strategy
walkthrough.text: Drawing from the face-up tray lets you see exactly what you are getting — you can pick a bird that fits your bonus card, matches food you already have, or fills a habitat you want to build up. Drawing from the deck is a gamble, but sometimes nothing in the tray appeals to you. There is no hand limit in Wingspan, so do not worry about drawing too many cards. The more options in your hand, the more flexible your future turns become.

walkthrough.title: Quick Recap
walkthrough.text: Look at that — each player took a different action on their first turn. Red played a bird, Blue gained food, Green laid eggs, and Yellow drew cards. Those are all four actions in the game. There are no other action types. Every single turn for the rest of the game, you will pick one of these four. Now play continues clockwise. Player 1 (Red) is up again for another turn, and everyone keeps going around until all eight action cubes have been used.

walkthrough.title: Brown Powers Kick In
walkthrough.text: Now the engine starts to shine. On Red's second turn, Red decides to gain food. Red places an action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in their forest row and resolves it — gaining one food from the birdfeeder, just like Blue did earlier. But now something extra happens. After resolving the base action, Red activates brown "When Activated" powers on birds in the forest row from right to left. Red's forest bird from Step 2 has a brown power that says something like "Gain 1 invertebrate from the supply." That power triggers now, giving Red a bonus invertebrate on top of the food they already took from the feeder.

walkthrough.title: The Engine-Building Loop
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of Wingspan. Every bird you add to a row makes that row's action more powerful. Red gained food from the birdfeeder plus a bonus invertebrate from their bird's brown power — two food from one action cube. As you add more birds, the chain grows. A second forest bird might let Red cache a food card or gain yet another food token. By mid-game, a single Gain Food action can net you three or four food. The same principle applies to grassland eggs and wetland card draws. More birds in a row means more value per action.

walkthrough.title: Using All Eight Cubes
walkthrough.text: Play continues around the table. Each player takes turns placing one action cube at a time until everyone has used all eight. It does not matter if players finish at different times — once you are out of cubes, you are done for the round and you wait for others to finish. Once every player's cubes are spent, Round 1 is over. Time to score the round goal and prepare for Round 2.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Procedure
walkthrough.text: Here is what happens at the end of every round. First, everyone picks up their action cubes from their player mat. Second, check the Round 1 goal tile on the goal board. Each player counts how many qualifying items they have — maybe it is birds in a certain habitat, or birds with a certain nest type. Each player places one action cube on the goal board to mark their score. That cube is gone for the rest of the game, which is why you have one fewer cube each round. Third, discard all three face-up birds in the tray and deal three fresh ones. Finally, pass the first player token one seat clockwise. Round 2 begins with seven cubes per player.
walkthrough.image_caption: Goal board with round scoring spaces

walkthrough.title: Congratulations — You Played a Full Round!
walkthrough.text: Nice work! You just walked through every action in Wingspan: playing a bird, gaining food from the birdfeeder, laying eggs, and drawing bird cards. You saw how brown powers chain together to build your engine, and you learned the end-of-round cleanup. The rest of the game is three more rounds of the same actions — but with more birds on your mat, every turn gets more powerful. You are ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Before You Fly
walkthrough.text: Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help. First, check out the Strategy tab for more advanced tips on bird combinations and round-by-round planning. Second, the Appendix has a quick-reference guide for every bird power and special term — no more flipping through the rulebook. And most importantly — if you ever get stuck or have a question during the game, just tap Q&A and ask your personal GameMaster Guide. It knows every rule and can answer anything in seconds. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome to Round 1
bullets: All players set up with player mats, starting birds, food, bonus card, and 8 action cubes
Round 1 gives you the most cubes — 8 — so there is room to try everything
Play proceeds clockwise from the first player token holder

summary.title: Player 1 (Red) Plays a Bird
bullets: Choose a bird from your hand that matches a habitat row on your mat
Pay the bird's food cost (shown in upper left of the card)
Place the bird in column 1 of the matching row — column 1 has no egg cost

summary.title: What Just Happened
bullets: Play a Bird places the card in its matching habitat row
Column 1: free. Columns 2-3: 1 egg each. Columns 4-5: 2 eggs each
White powers trigger once when played; brown powers trigger on future habitat actions
2-for-1 conversion: when playing a bird, any 2 food tokens can substitute for any 1 food

summary.title: Player 2 (Blue) Gains Food
bullets: Place action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in the forest row
Take a die from the birdfeeder and collect the matching food token
Split-face dice let you choose one of the two food types shown

summary.title: How the Birdfeeder Works
bullets: Remove a die from the tray to gain its matching food
If all remaining dice show the same face OR the tray is empty, reroll all 5 dice
Sometimes take an unneeded die to trigger a reroll for better options

summary.title: Player 3 (Green) Lays Eggs
bullets: Place action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in the grassland row
Take egg miniatures from the supply and place them on your birds
Each bird has a printed egg limit — never exceed it

summary.title: Why Eggs Matter
bullets: Eggs are currency: required to play birds into columns 2-5
Eggs are points: each egg on a bird at game end is worth 1 VP
Dual role makes Lay Eggs valuable throughout the entire game

summary.title: Player 4 (Yellow) Draws Bird Cards
bullets: Place action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in the wetland row
Draw from the face-up bird tray or from the top of the deck
Refill the tray immediately when a face-up card is taken

summary.title: Card Draw Strategy
bullets: Tray draw: see what you get — pick birds that fit your plan
Deck draw: blind gamble, but useful when nothing in the tray appeals
No hand limit — more cards means more flexibility

summary.title: Quick Recap
bullets: Red played a bird, Blue gained food, Green laid eggs, Yellow drew cards
Those are the only four actions in the game
Play continues clockwise until all cubes are used

summary.title: Brown Powers Kick In
bullets: When you take a habitat action, resolve the base slot first
Then activate brown powers on birds in that row from right to left
Red gains food from the feeder AND triggers a forest bird's brown power for bonus food

summary.title: The Engine-Building Loop
bullets: More birds in a row = more value from each action cube
Forest birds compound food gains; grassland birds compound egg production
By mid-game, a single action can generate 3-4 resources

summary.title: Using All Eight Cubes
bullets: Each player places one cube per turn, going clockwise
Once all 8 cubes are placed, your round is done
Round ends when every player has used all their cubes

summary.title: End of Round Procedure
bullets: Pick up all action cubes from your player mat
Score the round goal — place 1 cube on the goal board (that cube is gone for good)
Discard all 3 face-up tray birds, deal 3 new ones
Pass first player token clockwise — Round 2 starts with 7 cubes

summary.title: Congratulations — You Played a Full Round!
bullets: You practiced all four actions: Play a Bird, Gain Food, Lay Eggs, Draw Cards
You saw brown powers chain together to amplify habitat actions
Three more rounds of the same actions — but every turn gets more powerful

summary.title: Before You Fly
bullets: Strategy tab — advanced tips and bird combos
Appendix — quick-reference for powers and terms
Ask your GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

scenarios.title: Food Engine Focus
scenarios.description: Build a forest engine where brown powers compound into massive food gains

walkthrough.title: The Forest Strategy
walkthrough.text: In this scenario, we are going to follow one player — Player 1 (Red) — as they build a food engine over the first two rounds. The forest strategy is one of the strongest openings in Wingspan because food is the universal resource. You need it to play birds, and the more birds you play, the more food you generate. It is a snowball that starts small and gets huge. Let's see it in action.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat with an empty forest row ready to fill

walkthrough.title: Playing a Cheap Forest Bird
walkthrough.text: Round 1, Turn 1. Red looks at their starting hand and finds a forest bird that costs just one seed. Red pays the seed token and places the bird in column 1 of the forest row — no egg cost for column 1. This bird has a brown power: "When Activated: gain 1 invertebrate from the supply." It is not flashy yet, but every time Red takes the Gain Food action, this power will fire automatically. That is free food on top of whatever Red takes from the birdfeeder.

walkthrough.title: Gaining Food with One Bird
walkthrough.text: A couple of turns later, Red takes the Gain Food action. Red places an action cube on the leftmost exposed forest slot and takes a die from the birdfeeder — let's say a fruit token. Then Red activates the brown power on their forest bird from right to left. The bird grants one invertebrate from the supply. So Red spent one action cube and gained two food: one fruit from the feeder and one invertebrate from the power. Not bad for one bird.
walkthrough.image_caption: Taking a die from the birdfeeder

walkthrough.title: Playing a Second Forest Bird
walkthrough.text: Red now has plenty of food. On their next turn, Red plays a second forest bird. This one costs one invertebrate and one seed — affordable with Red's growing supply. Red places it in column 2 of the forest row, paying the food cost plus one egg for the column 2 slot. This second bird has a brown power: "When Activated: all players gain 1 seed from the supply, and you gain 1 additional seed." Now Red has two brown powers stacked in the forest.

walkthrough.title: Gaining Food with Two Birds
walkthrough.text: Next time Red takes the Gain Food action, watch what happens. Red places the cube, takes one food die from the birdfeeder — that is one food. Then the brown powers fire from right to left. Bird 2 triggers: everyone gains one seed, and Red gains an extra seed — that is two seeds for Red. Bird 1 triggers: Red gains one invertebrate from the supply. Grand total from one action cube: one food from the feeder, two seeds, and one invertebrate. Four food tokens from a single action.

walkthrough.title: Why Forest-First is Strong
walkthrough.text: Here is the strategic insight. Food is the gatekeeper resource. You cannot play birds without it, and you cannot build your grassland or wetland engines without birds. By loading up the forest row first, Red ensures they will never be food-starved. Every Gain Food action gets more efficient, which means Red can afford expensive birds that other players might struggle to pay for. It is the classic engine-building snowball: food lets you play birds, birds generate more food.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Adding a Third Forest Bird
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins with seven cubes. Red has a hand full of food tokens and plays a third forest bird into column 3 of the forest row. Column 3 costs one egg, which Red laid on a previous turn. This third bird has a brown power: "When Activated: look at the top card of the deck. If it is a forest bird, play it for free." Now Red has three brown powers chaining together every time they gain food.

walkthrough.title: The Full Chain in Action
walkthrough.text: Red takes the Gain Food action again. Action cube goes down. One food from the birdfeeder. Then right to left: Bird 3 triggers — Red peeks at the top deck card and might play a free bird. Bird 2 triggers — everyone gets a seed, Red gets two. Bird 1 triggers — Red gets an invertebrate. From one action cube, Red is gaining a pile of food and possibly a free bird. This is what a mature food engine looks like.

walkthrough.title: Spending the Wealth
walkthrough.text: With all this food, Red can afford to branch out. Red starts playing expensive birds in the grassland and wetland rows — birds with high food costs that other players might skip because they cannot afford them. These birds often have high point values printed on the card. Remember, the number in the top right of each bird card is its base victory points. Expensive birds tend to be worth more points. Red's food engine is now funding a high-scoring bird collection.

walkthrough.title: The 2-for-1 Conversion Rule
walkthrough.text: One more trick to know. When playing a bird, if you are missing one specific food type, you can use the 2-for-1 conversion: spend any two food tokens to count as one food of any type. This only works when playing birds, not at any other time. With Red's overflowing food supply, this conversion is easy to use. Two extra invertebrates can become the fish you are missing. Surplus food is never wasted.

walkthrough.title: Watch Out For These Pitfalls
walkthrough.text: The forest strategy is not without risks. If you spend too many turns gaining food and not enough playing birds, you can fall behind on eggs and end-of-round goals. Make sure you play birds consistently — food sitting unused in your supply is zero points at the end. Also watch the birdfeeder. If other players are not taking dice, you might get stuck with food types you do not need. Use the reroll rule to your advantage: if all remaining dice match, you get a fresh roll.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Reminder
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, food tokens cached on bird cards score one point each, but food in your personal supply does not score anything. Some forest birds have powers that let you cache food from the supply onto the card — those are extra valuable because they convert food into points. Keep an eye out for birds with caching powers. They turn your food engine into a points engine.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations — You Built a Food Engine!
walkthrough.text: Great job! You just saw how stacking brown powers in the forest row turns a simple Gain Food action into a resource-generating machine. The core loop is straightforward: play cheap forest birds early, gain food to fund expensive birds later, and use caching powers to convert surplus food into victory points. This is one of the most reliable strategies in Wingspan, and it gives you a strong foundation no matter what else happens at the table.

walkthrough.title: Ready for More?
walkthrough.text: Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help. First, check out the Strategy tab for more advanced tips on bird combinations and round-by-round planning. Second, the Appendix has a quick-reference guide for every bird power and special term — no more flipping through the rulebook. And most importantly — if you ever get stuck or have a question during the game, just tap Q&A and ask your personal GameMaster Guide. It knows every rule and can answer anything in seconds. Have fun!

summary.title: The Forest Strategy
bullets: Focus on playing birds in the forest row to build a food-generating engine
Food is the gatekeeper resource — you need it to play every bird
The snowball effect: more forest birds = more food per action cube

summary.title: Playing a Cheap Forest Bird
bullets: Start with low-cost forest birds (1 food, column 1 = no egg cost)
Look for brown "When Activated" powers that grant bonus food
Column 1 placement is free — no egg cost required

summary.title: Gaining Food with One Bird
bullets: Gain Food action: take a die from the birdfeeder, then activate brown powers right to left
One forest bird adds roughly 1 bonus food per Gain Food action
Two food from one action cube is a solid early return

summary.title: Playing a Second Forest Bird
bullets: Column 2 costs the bird's food price plus 1 egg
Stack complementary brown powers for multiplicative gains
Shared-benefit powers (all players gain food) still net you more than opponents

summary.title: Gaining Food with Two Birds
bullets: Brown powers fire right to left after the base Gain Food action
Two stacked birds can yield 3-4 food from a single action cube
Each new forest bird makes every future Gain Food action stronger

summary.title: Why Forest-First is Strong
bullets: Food funds everything — birds, expansions, engine pieces
Early food surplus means you can afford expensive, high-point birds later
Classic snowball: food → birds → more food → bigger birds

summary.title: Round 2 — Adding a Third Forest Bird
bullets: Column 3 costs the bird's food price plus 1 egg
Three brown powers chaining together is the engine at full speed
Look for powers that play free birds or cache food for points

summary.title: The Full Chain in Action
bullets: One Gain Food action now triggers three brown powers sequentially
Potential output: birdfeeder food + bonus food tokens + a free bird
This is a mature engine — every action cube delivers outsized value

summary.title: Spending the Wealth
bullets: Use surplus food to play expensive high-VP birds in grassland and wetland
Bird point values are printed in the top right of each card
Diversifying rows also helps with end-of-round goal scoring

summary.title: The 2-for-1 Conversion Rule
bullets: When playing a bird only, any 2 food tokens can substitute for 1 food of any type
Only applies to the Play a Bird action, not other food spending
Surplus food is never wasted when you have this conversion available

summary.title: Watch Out For These Pitfalls
bullets: Do not hoard food without playing birds — unspent food scores zero
Balance food actions with bird plays to keep your engine growing
Use the birdfeeder reroll rule when dice get stuck on one face

summary.title: Final Scoring Reminder
bullets: Food in your personal supply scores nothing at game end
Food cached on bird cards scores 1 VP each
Caching powers convert your food engine directly into victory points

summary.title: Congratulations — You Built a Food Engine!
bullets: Stacking brown powers in the forest row is one of the most reliable strategies
Core loop: cheap forest birds → gain food → fund expensive birds → cache for points
This foundation supports any late-game pivot into eggs or card draw

summary.title: Ready for More?
bullets: Strategy tab — advanced tips and bird combos
Appendix — quick-reference for powers and terms
Ask your GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

scenarios.title: Egg Machine
scenarios.description: Build a grassland engine that turns eggs into currency and victory points

walkthrough.title: The Grassland Strategy
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the egg machine. In this scenario, we are following Player 1 (Red) as they focus on the grassland row to generate a steady stream of eggs. Eggs are one of the most versatile resources in Wingspan — you need them to play birds into columns 2 through 5, and every egg left on a bird at the end of the game is worth one victory point. A strong grassland engine gives you both the currency to expand and the points to win.
walkthrough.image_caption: Egg supply ready for a grassland-focused game

walkthrough.title: Playing a Grassland Bird with High Egg Limit
walkthrough.text: Round 1, Turn 1. Red picks a grassland bird from their hand. The key stat to look for is the egg limit — the number printed on the nest icon at the bottom of the card. Red chooses a bird with an egg limit of 5, meaning it can hold up to five eggs over the course of the game. Red pays its food cost and places it in column 1 of the grassland row. Column 1 is free, no egg cost. This bird has a brown power: "When Activated: lay 1 egg on any bird."

walkthrough.title: First Lay Eggs Action
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Red takes the Lay Eggs action. Red places an action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in the grassland row, which says "Lay 2 eggs." Red takes two eggs from the supply and places them on any birds they have — both go onto the grassland bird with the big egg limit. Then the brown power fires: the grassland bird's power says lay one additional egg on any bird. That is three eggs from one action cube. Red's grassland bird now holds three of its five possible eggs.
walkthrough.image_caption: Grassland row with action cube placed

walkthrough.title: Eggs as Currency
walkthrough.text: Now Red has eggs to spend. On the next turn, Red plays a second bird — this time a forest bird that costs one seed and goes into column 2 of the forest row. Column 2 requires one egg as a placement cost. Red takes one egg off any bird on their mat and returns it to the supply. It does not matter which bird the egg came from. Red is now using the grassland engine to fuel expansion into other habitats. This is the currency side of eggs at work.

walkthrough.title: Adding a Second Grassland Bird
walkthrough.text: Back in the grassland, Red plays a second grassland bird into column 2 of the grassland row. This costs one egg for the column slot plus the bird's food cost. Red pays up. This bird has an egg limit of 4 and a brown power: "When Activated: each player lays 1 egg on a bird. You lay 1 egg on any bird." Now Red has two grassland birds with brown powers that will chain together every time Red takes the Lay Eggs action.

walkthrough.title: The Egg Snowball
walkthrough.text: Red takes the Lay Eggs action again. The base slot says "Lay 2 eggs." Then brown powers fire right to left. Bird 2 triggers: everyone lays one egg, and Red lays one egg. Bird 1 triggers: Red lays one more egg on any bird. Total for Red: two base eggs, plus one from Bird 2, plus one from Bird 1 — four eggs from a single action cube. The egg machine is humming. Red can spread these eggs across multiple birds to stay under each bird's egg limit.

walkthrough.title: The Dual-Use Calculation
walkthrough.text: Here is the strategic tension that makes the egg strategy interesting. Every egg on a bird at game end is worth one victory point. But every egg you spend to play a bird into columns 2-5 is an egg that will not score. The trick is to lay more eggs than you spend. With four eggs per Lay Eggs action, Red can afford to spend one or two on bird placements and still stockpile the rest for end-game scoring. High egg-limit birds are the key — they hold more eggs and score more points.

walkthrough.title: Playing Expensive, High-Point Birds
walkthrough.text: With a steady egg supply, Red can now play birds into columns 4 and 5, which cost two eggs each. These deeper columns are where the most powerful birds often land. Red plays a high-value wetland bird — 9 victory points printed on the card — into column 4 of the wetland row, paying two eggs and its food cost. That bird alone might score more than several smaller birds combined. The egg engine made this possible.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Egg Scoring
walkthrough.text: As the game nears its final rounds, Red shifts strategy. Instead of spending eggs on bird placements, Red focuses purely on the Lay Eggs action to stack eggs onto every bird on the mat. Each bird with a high egg limit becomes a little scoring platform. If Red has five birds with an average of three eggs each, that is fifteen bonus victory points from eggs alone. Combined with the birds' printed point values, bonus card scoring, and round goals, the egg strategy can produce very competitive totals.

walkthrough.title: Tips for the Egg Strategy
walkthrough.text: A few things to keep in mind. First, prioritize birds with high egg limits — anything with a 5 or 6 egg limit is gold for this strategy. Second, do not neglect the forest row entirely. You still need food to play birds, so spend a few early turns gaining food before committing to the grassland. Third, watch for bonus cards that reward eggs or grassland birds — they synergize perfectly with this approach. And fourth, remember that star nests are wild and count for any nest-type goals.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations — You Built an Egg Machine!
walkthrough.text: Well done! You just walked through a grassland-focused strategy that turns eggs into both currency and victory points. The core loop is simple but effective: play grassland birds with high egg limits and brown egg-laying powers, use the Lay Eggs action to generate a surplus, spend some eggs to play high-value birds elsewhere, and stockpile the rest for end-game scoring. It is a reliable strategy that scales beautifully across all four rounds.

walkthrough.title: What's Next?
walkthrough.text: Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help. First, check out the Strategy tab for more advanced tips on bird combinations and round-by-round planning. Second, the Appendix has a quick-reference guide for every bird power and special term — no more flipping through the rulebook. And most importantly — if you ever get stuck or have a question during the game, just tap Q&A and ask your personal GameMaster Guide. It knows every rule and can answer anything in seconds. Have fun!

summary.title: The Grassland Strategy
bullets: Grassland row focus generates eggs for currency and end-game points
Eggs are needed to play birds into columns 2-5 (1 egg for cols 2-3, 2 eggs for cols 4-5)
Every egg left on a bird at game end is worth 1 VP

summary.title: Playing a Grassland Bird with High Egg Limit
bullets: Prioritize birds with egg limits of 4, 5, or 6
Column 1 placement is free — no egg cost
Brown powers that lay bonus eggs are the engine's fuel

summary.title: First Lay Eggs Action
bullets: Place action cube on leftmost exposed grassland slot
Lay the base number of eggs, then activate brown powers right to left
With one brown-power bird: 2 base + 1 power = 3 eggs per action

summary.title: Eggs as Currency
bullets: Spend eggs to play birds into columns 2-5 of any habitat row
Remove eggs from any bird on your mat — your choice
Eggs fund expansion into forest and wetland rows

summary.title: Adding a Second Grassland Bird
bullets: Column 2 costs the bird's food plus 1 egg for placement
Stack brown powers for compounding egg gains
Shared-benefit powers still net you more eggs than opponents

summary.title: The Egg Snowball
bullets: Two grassland birds: 2 base + 1 from Bird 2 + 1 from Bird 1 = 4 eggs per action
Spread eggs across birds to stay under each bird's egg limit
Each new grassland bird makes every future Lay Eggs action stronger

summary.title: The Dual-Use Calculation
bullets: Eggs spent on placement do not score — eggs kept on birds do score
Lay more eggs than you spend to stay in the positive
High egg-limit birds maximize scoring potential

summary.title: Playing Expensive, High-Point Birds
bullets: Columns 4-5 cost 2 eggs each but unlock powerful bird slots
Egg surplus lets you afford high-VP birds other players cannot
A single 9-point bird can outweigh several smaller ones

summary.title: End-Game Egg Scoring
bullets: In later rounds, shift from spending eggs to stockpiling them
5 birds averaging 3 eggs each = 15 bonus VP from eggs alone
Combines with bird points, bonus cards, and round goals for competitive totals

summary.title: Tips for the Egg Strategy
bullets: Target birds with egg limits of 5-6 for maximum scoring platforms
Do not neglect early food — you need it to play the grassland birds
Star nests are wild and count for any nest-type goals
Bonus cards rewarding eggs or grassland birds synergize perfectly

summary.title: Congratulations — You Built an Egg Machine!
bullets: Core loop: grassland birds → lay eggs → spend some to expand → stockpile the rest
High egg-limit birds with brown powers are the foundation
Strategy scales well across all four rounds

summary.title: What's Next?
bullets: Strategy tab — advanced tips and bird combos
Appendix — quick-reference for powers and terms
Ask your GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

scenarios.title: Card Draw Engine
scenarios.description: Build a wetland engine that keeps your hand full and your options wide open

walkthrough.title: The Wetland Strategy
walkthrough.text: In this scenario, we follow Player 1 (Red) as they build a card draw engine in the wetland row. The wetland strategy is about information and flexibility. While other players scramble to find the right bird, Red always has a hand full of options. More cards means Red can pick the perfect bird for every situation, pivot strategies on the fly, and take advantage of tucking and caching mechanics for bonus points. Let's see how it works.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bird tray with cards available for drawing

walkthrough.title: Playing a Wetland Bird with a Draw Power
walkthrough.text: Round 1, Turn 1. Red selects a wetland bird from their starting hand. This bird costs one fish and has a brown power: "When Activated: draw 1 card." Red pays the fish token and places the bird in column 1 of the wetland row. No egg cost for column 1. The bird also has a white "When Played" power that says "Draw 2 cards." Since white powers trigger once when the bird is played, Red immediately draws two cards from the deck. Red's hand just grew before the engine even started.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Cards with a Brown Power
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Red takes the Draw Bird Cards action. Red places an action cube on the leftmost exposed slot in the wetland row, which says "Draw 1 card." Red draws a card from the tray — a nice grassland bird with a high point value. Then the brown power fires: the wetland bird's power says draw one additional card. Red draws one more from the deck. Two cards from one action cube, plus Red got to be selective with the first draw by picking from the tray.

walkthrough.title: Hand Size Equals Flexibility
walkthrough.text: Here is why card draw is so powerful. Most players have two or three birds in hand at any given time. Red has six or seven. When the end-of-round goal is about bowl nests, Red can probably find a bowl-nest bird in hand. When a perfect forest bird shows up in the tray, Red already has the food for it because they planned ahead. Wingspan has no hand limit, so there is no penalty for holding lots of cards. Every card is a potential play, and having more choices means making better decisions.

walkthrough.title: Adding a Second Wetland Bird
walkthrough.text: Red plays a second wetland bird into column 2 of the wetland row, paying one egg and the bird's food cost. This bird has a brown power: "When Activated: tuck 1 card from your hand behind this bird. If you do, draw 1 card." Tucking is a special mechanic — the tucked card goes face down behind the bird and is worth one victory point at game end. It is like converting an unwanted card into a point. And Red gets to draw a replacement card, so their hand stays full.

walkthrough.title: The Tuck-and-Draw Loop
walkthrough.text: Red takes the Draw Bird Cards action again. Base action: draw one card. Then brown powers fire right to left. Bird 2: Red tucks a card from their hand behind this bird — one future VP — and draws a replacement. Bird 1: Red draws one more card. Net result from one action cube: Red draws three cards and scores one future VP from the tucked card. The hand stays large, the point total quietly grows, and Red has even more options for their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Caching for Points
walkthrough.text: Some wetland birds have powers that let you cache food tokens from the supply onto the bird card. Each cached food is worth one VP at game end, just like tucked cards. If Red finds a bird with a caching power, those bonus points add up fast. Between tucked cards and cached food, Red's wetland birds can each be worth their printed point value plus three, four, or even five bonus points from stored items. The card draw engine is secretly a points engine.

walkthrough.title: Playing Birds Everywhere
walkthrough.text: With a packed hand, Red starts placing birds across all three habitats. Red finds the perfect forest bird to round out their food supply, a grassland bird with a high egg limit for scoring, and another wetland bird to keep the draw engine growing. Other players are stuck with whatever they happen to draw — Red gets to choose. This is the true advantage of card draw: not just having cards, but having the right cards at the right time.

walkthrough.title: Fueling the Rest of Your Engine
walkthrough.text: The wetland strategy pairs beautifully with the other two rows. Draw extra cards, then play the best birds in your forest and grassland rows. A well-stocked hand means you are never stuck wasting a turn on a bad bird play. In the late game, you can shift to pure tucking — use the Draw Cards action not for the cards themselves, but for the tucked-card victory points that come along for the ride. Every action cube pulls double duty.

walkthrough.title: Tips for the Card Draw Strategy
walkthrough.text: Keep these in mind. First, do not hold cards just to hold them — play birds regularly to build your mat. Cards in hand score zero points. Second, look for birds with tucking powers — they are the main way the wetland strategy generates bonus points. Third, remember that when you draw from the tray, you see what you get, but the tray only refills after you finish drawing. So if you draw one from the tray and one from the deck, the tray card is not replaced until your entire action resolves. Fourth, pink "Once Between Turns" powers on wetland birds can trigger during opponents' turns, giving you draws or tucks even when it is not your turn.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations — You Built a Card Draw Engine!
walkthrough.text: Excellent work! You just walked through a wetland-focused strategy built on card draw, tucking, and flexibility. The core loop is draw cards to keep your options open, tuck unwanted cards for bonus VP, and use your deep hand to play the perfect bird in every situation. The card draw engine does not generate as many immediate resources as the forest or grassland, but it gives you something equally valuable — the power to always have the right answer.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn to Soar
walkthrough.text: Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help. First, check out the Strategy tab for more advanced tips on bird combinations and round-by-round planning. Second, the Appendix has a quick-reference guide for every bird power and special term — no more flipping through the rulebook. And most importantly — if you ever get stuck or have a question during the game, just tap Q&A and ask your personal GameMaster Guide. It knows every rule and can answer anything in seconds. Have fun!

summary.title: The Wetland Strategy
bullets: Wetland row focus builds a card draw engine for maximum flexibility
More cards in hand means better decisions and the ability to pivot strategies
Tucking and caching mechanics convert extra cards into bonus VP

summary.title: Playing a Wetland Bird with a Draw Power
bullets: Start with a low-cost wetland bird with a brown draw power
White "When Played" powers trigger once immediately for instant card advantage
Column 1 is free — no egg cost required

summary.title: Drawing Cards with a Brown Power
bullets: Draw Cards action: take from tray or deck, then activate brown powers right to left
One brown-power bird: 1 base + 1 power = 2 cards per action
Drawing from the tray lets you pick the best available bird

summary.title: Hand Size Equals Flexibility
bullets: No hand limit in Wingspan — hold as many cards as you like
Large hand lets you respond to round goals and adapt to the board
More choices = better decisions on every turn

summary.title: Adding a Second Wetland Bird
bullets: Column 2 costs the bird's food price plus 1 egg
Tucking powers: place a card face down behind the bird for 1 VP each at game end
Tuck-and-draw powers keep your hand full while scoring points

summary.title: The Tuck-and-Draw Loop
bullets: Two wetland birds: 1 base draw + 1 tuck/draw + 1 draw = 3 cards and 1 VP per action
Tucked cards score 1 VP each at game end
Hand stays full while bonus points quietly accumulate

summary.title: Caching for Points
bullets: Some birds cache food tokens onto the card — each cached food = 1 VP
Between tucking and caching, a single bird can score 4-5 bonus VP
The draw engine doubles as a points engine

summary.title: Playing Birds Everywhere
bullets: A large hand lets you play the perfect bird in every habitat
Other players take what they can get — you choose what you want
Flexibility is the true advantage of the card draw approach

summary.title: Fueling the Rest of Your Engine
bullets: Draw cards, then play the best birds across forest and grassland
Late game: shift to pure tucking for VP generation
Every Draw Cards action pulls double duty — cards and points

summary.title: Tips for the Card Draw Strategy
bullets: Play birds regularly — cards in hand score zero points
Tucking powers are the main VP generator for this strategy
Tray cards are not replaced until your entire draw action resolves
Pink powers on wetland birds can trigger on opponents' turns for bonus draws

summary.title: Congratulations — You Built a Card Draw Engine!
bullets: Core loop: draw cards → tuck for VP → play the perfect bird every time
Wetland engine trades raw resources for information and flexibility
Tucked cards and cached food turn card advantage into victory points
